segments.s682-g33: بدین کار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by/with this deed' (بدین = 'with this'); پشت here means 'support' or 'protection' rather than the bodily 'back'. The clause follows the simple past copular pattern 'پشت ... بود' = '... was the support', so یزدان بود is a past-tense nominal predicate 'was God'.
segments.s682-g34: همآواز تو is an adverbial/phrase meaning 'in unison/accord with you' (literally 'together-voiced with you'), placed before the subject بخت. خندان is an adjective modifying بخت ('smiling/cheerful fortune') and بود is the past copula 'was'. The overall order is adverbial — subject — adjective — copula.
segments.s682-g35: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بگذاشت is a third-person past-form (preterite) meaning 'he left/abandoned/let go' in context. خواهی is a second-person modal/volitional 'you will/you wish' and همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed/ever'); مرز وبوم is the compound object 'border and homeland'. The clause mixes a temporal clause with a directive/conditional turn addressed to 'you'.
segments.s682-g36: از ایدر is a prepositional phrase 'from here' (از = 'from', ایدر = 'here'). برو is the imperative 'go' directed at 'you'. تازیان is the destination 'the Arabs' and تا بروم adds a temporal/goal clause 'until I go' or 'as far as my going', with تا functioning as 'until/as far as' and بروم a 1st-person present/future-subjunctive 'I go'.
segments.s682-g37: سخنهای این بنده is a genitive construction 'the words of this servant' where بنده is a humble self-reference. چاره جوی is a noun + present-participial compound meaning 'seeking a remedy' (چاره 'remedy' + جوی 'seeker/searcher'), functioning as an adjectival phrase modifying بنده or سخنهای.
segments.s682-g38: چو رفتی is a temporal clause 'when you go/when you have gone' (چو = 'when', رفتی = 2nd-person past/perfect 'you went' used here with future/imperative force). یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' and بقیصر is a prepositional/directional phrase 'to/for the Caesar'. بگوی in the manuscript form is a verb meaning 'tell' (imperative/subjunctive), here introducing the instruction to report the words.
segments.s682-g39: بجایی که is a relative/adverbial phrase 'to/at the place where'. دین است is a simple clause 'there is religion' (دین as noun, است copula). و هم links an additional predicate and وخواستست is a fused verb form 'and he wanted/and he demanded' (an archaic/poetic compound used as a past-tense claim). The clause sets a situation ('where religion is and he desired').
segments.s682-g40: سلیح و سپاه وی is a coordinated noun phrase 'his arms and his army' (سلیح 'weapons', سپاه 'army', وی = 'his'). آراستست is a poetic/past copula form meaning 'are arrayed/are adorned/are prepared' (آراسته + است contracted), giving the resultative predicate about his forces.
segments.s682-g97: چو functions as a temporal conjunction 'when' here, followed by او ('he') and the past verb برگذشت (3rd pl./sg. past) meaning 'passed by'. این دو بیدادگر is a noun phrase 'these two bēdādgar(s)' (= 'the two tyrants/cruel ones') placed after the clause; word order is typical of classical Persian where the time-clause comes first and the descriptive noun phrase follows.
segments.s682-g98: ازو (from him) precedes the verb بازگشتند ('they returned/turned back' — 3rd pl. past). پر کینه سر is a compact adjectival phrase: پر ('full (of)') + کینه ('hatred') modifying سر ('head'); literally 'heads full of hatred' — a poetic inversion placing the modifier before the noun.
segments.s682-g99: زراه is a prepositional phrase 'from the road' (z- = 'from' + راه 'road'), اندر 'into/in' introduces the place ایوان ('iwan/porch, audience hall') and شاه ('of the king') is a genitive modifier of ایوان. آمدند is the 3rd person plural past 'they came/arrived'; Persian places the location phrase before the verb.
segments.s682-g100: پراز ('full of') takes the following noun رنج ('suffering') to form 'full of suffering'. The conjunction و links the next coordinated phrase دل پرگناه — literally 'heart sinful' = 'with sinful heart(s)'. آمدند repeats the past-plural verb 'they came', giving a rhythmic, parallel description.
segments.s682-g101: ز (poetic 'from') + در ('gate') gives 'from the gate'; چون here functions as 'when' introducing the temporal clause, رسیدند is 3rd pl. past 'they arrived', and نزدیک تخت ('near the throne') is the locative phrase describing where they arrived. Classical order places the temporal marker and the place phrase around the verb.
segments.s682-g102: زهی is an interjection/exclamation (here 'zehî!' — an archaic shout). از کمان ('from the bow') with باز کردند (compound past verb: باز 'open/loose' + کردند 'they did') conveys that they loosed/drew their bows; سخت is an adverb 'firmly/strongly' modifying the action. Word order places the exclamation before the action and the adverb after the verb cluster.
segments.s682-g103: فگندند is the past plural 'they threw/cast' (the direct object — e.g., a noose — is not stated but implied); ناگاه 'suddenly' is an adverb modifying the verb. در گردنش literally 'on/around his neck' uses در + گردن + the enclitic possessive ش ('his') to indicate the locus of the action.
segments.s682-g104: بیاویختند is the past plural 'they hung' or 'they suspended'; آن is the demonstrative 'that/the', گرامی 'honored/precious' qualifies تن 'body', and the enclitic ش marks possession 'his'. Together this is 'they hanged that noble body of his' — the object of the hanging verb.
segments.s682-g105: شد is the past/copular verb 'became/was made' placed before its nominal complement — a common poetic inversion. آن تاج و آن تخت شاهنشهان is a coordinated noun phrase 'that crown and that throne of the shahanshahs (emperors)', with the demonstrative آن repeated for emphasis and شاهنشهان in the genitive/attributive relation 'of the emperors'.
segments.s682-g106: توگفتی is the 2nd-person past 'you said'; که introduces the reported content ('that'). هرمز is the proper name (here Ahura Mazda / Hurmuz) and نبد is a negative existential/past 'was not / did not exist'. درجهان here functions as the adverbial 'in the world' (poetic orthography), completing the reported clause.
segments.s682-g107: چنین است is a present statement 'thus is' (establishing a general truth). آیین گردنده دهر is a nominal phrase 'the rule/custom of the turning world' where گردنده is a present-participial modifier 'turning/rotating' and دهر ('age, time') names the domain governed by that custom.
segments.s682-g108: گهی ... گاه ... is a parallel pair meaning 'sometimes ... at other times'. نوش here denotes pleasurable drink/delight (not merely the physical beverage) and بار آورد is the verb phrase 'brings/carries as a load' (i.e., produces or bestows). زهر in the final position contrasts with نوش and means 'poison'.
segments.s682-g109: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. مایه اینست is an inverted copular clause 'this is the source/ground' (مایه = cause/source). سودش مجوی is an imperative/prohibitive 'do not seek its profit' — مجوی is the negative imperative/subjunctive form meaning 'do not seek'.
segments.s682-g110: که here gives the reason 'for/because'. درجستنش is the verbal noun phrase 'in seeking it' (در + جستن + enclitic object -ش). رنجت آید بروی is a small clause 'hardship will come upon you' where رنجت is the patient/experiencer, آید is the verb 'will come/happen', and بروی functions as a classical verbal adjunct meaning 'befall/come upon you'.
segments.s682-g111: چوشد is the temporal subordinating particle 'چو شد' = 'when/when it happens that'. گردش روز is 'the turning (wheel) of days' or 'the course of time'. هرمز بپای here indicates the person whose lot or turn the turning reaches — poetically 'falls to Hurmuz' (i.e., when Hurmuz's time arrives).
segments.s682-g112: تهی ماند is 'remained empty' (adjective + past verb). زان تخت فرخنده جای is a genitival/attributive phrase 'the place of that auspicious/fortunate throne' (زان = 'that/thereof'), so the whole clause says that the place/seat of that fortunate throne is left empty.
segments.s682-g25: گر at the start is the conditional 'if'. ای دون is a vocative ('O base/inferior one'). که introduces a subordinate clause; فرمان دهد is a classical-style finite clause meaning 'gives/orders' (دهد is the present/subjunctive/optative form of دادن used here to report a possible/future action). شهریار is the logical subject 'the sovereign/king' placed after the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s682-g26: سواران تازی is a noun phrase 'Arab horsemen/riders' (modifier after head, as is common in poetry). برم is the first-person singular present/subjunctive of بردن used here with a prospective/optative sense 'I will bring/send'; the verb comes after its object phrase in this line. بی‌شمار is a postpositive adjective 'countless'.
segments.s682-g27: بدو گفت is an expression 'he said to him' with the dative/locative prepositional element بدو ('to him') preceding the verb گفت; هرمز is the speaker's name placed after the verb (poetic inversion). که introduces indirect speech. این رای نیست is a negated predicate 'this counsel/plan is not (so)' — رای here means 'plan/counsel'.
segments.s682-g28: This subordinate clause (introduced again by که) uses an existential/possessive construction: اکنون 'now', تو را marks the person affected ('you' as possessor/experiencer), and پای برجای نیست literally 'a foot in place is not' → idiomatically 'you have no firm footing/standing now'. Note the verbless-looking structure becomes negative with نیست at the end.
segments.s682-g29: نباشند is a negative optative/imperative in the third person plural 'may they not be' or 'let them not be'. یاور تو را is 'helpers/allies for you' (تو را marks the person who would benefit), and تازیان is the subject 'the Arabs' placed after the predicate for emphasis (poetic inversion).
segments.s682-g30: چوجایی ('wherever' / 'in whatever place') fronted as an adverbial; نبینند is the negative present/subjunctive plural 'they do not see/consider/perceive'; سود و زیان 'profit and loss' is the object of نبینند — together: 'wherever they do not regard profit and loss.'
segments.s682-g31: بدرد دل اندر is a prepositional phrase meaning roughly 'into/for the pain of the heart' (بدرد here used in the sense 'to cause pain / concerning pain'), and تو را marks the possessor 'your'. زار نیز ('wretched, too') modifies that inner heart/state — together: 'your wretched heart as well.' The word order is compact and typical of classical Persian: modifier + possessor + object marker.
segments.s682-g32: سپارند is a third-person plural verb 'they entrust/hand over'. بدشمن is the indirect object 'to a hostile/evil enemy' and precedes the verb for emphasis. از بهر is a compound prepositional phrase 'for the sake of / because of' and چیز ('thing') indicates a (perhaps trivial) cause — 'they hand it over to an enemy for the sake of a thing/trifle.'
segments.s682-g17: همی is a habitual/continuous particle ('kept/ever'), placed before the verb. شاه is the noun 'king' (predicate noun). خوانند is 3rd-person plural present/habitual (they call), here meaning 'they call' (چنان خواندن = to call someone X). بهرام را uses the proper name with the object marker را (marks it as the definite object of خوانند). Word order is particle + predicate noun + verb + object (poetic order).
segments.s682-g18: ندیدند is simple past, 3rd-person plural negative 'they did not see'. آغاز فرجام را is a noun phrase 'the beginning of the end' with فرجام governed by آغاز and definite object marked by را; the verb negates the whole noun phrase. In poetry the object-marker را follows the whole phrase.
segments.s682-g19: پس 'so/then' begins the clause; من کنون is 'I now' (subject + temporal adverb). تا here functions as 'as far as' (spatial extent) governing the following place phrase پل نهروان 'the Nahrawan bridge'. The verb of motion is omitted here (a common poetic ellipsis), so this segment establishes the speaker, time and limit/destination.
segments.s682-g20: بیاورد is 3rd-person singular present/habitual/narrative 'brings' or 'comes' (here narrative present: 'the army comes/arrives'). لشکر is the subject (army) even though it follows the verb — V‑S order is common in Persian poetry. چو is the comparative particle 'like/as', introducing the simile کوهی گران 'a heavy mountain' (کوهی = mountain-like / mountain, گران = heavy).
segments.s682-g21: چوشد is a subordinating particle/verb form meaning 'when it happened that' / 'when it became'. کاربی is a fused phrase meaning 'کارِ بی' (work/affair without — here 'fruitless/useless'); برگ 'leaf' is literal but used idiomatically with بی to imply barrenness. بگریختم is 1st-person past 'I fled' (ب- prefix + preterite). Together: 'When the matter became barren/fruitless, I fled.'
segments.s682-g22: بدام here is an archaic/poetic prepositional phrase contracted from به دام ('into the snare/trap'); بلا is 'calamity'. در + نیاویختم: نیاویختم is the past negative of آویختن (to cling/attach), literally 'I did not cling/attach (myself)'; idiomatically 'I did not get caught/ensnared'. The whole phrase means 'I did not fall/get caught into the snare of calamity.' (بدام is lexicalized in older Persian as 'into the trap').
segments.s682-g23: نگه کردم is a light verbal construction: نگه (gaze/consideration) + کردم (I did) => 'I considered / I weighed'. اکنون 'now' is a temporal adverb. به سود و زیان is a prepositional phrase 'with regard to profit and loss' or 'the profit and the loss' (به marking the domain of consideration). The verb کرده forms a past-tense perfective sense here.
segments.s682-g24: نباشند is a 3rd-person plural negative form of بودن used impersonally/existentially 'there are not / there is no'. یاور 'helper/ally' is the noun negated. مگر is the exception particle 'except', and تازیان 'the Arabs' is the noun in exception. Word order places the verb before the noun for emphasis/poetic rhythm.
segments.s682-g41: فریدونیان is a plural proper/tribal name ('the Fereydunids'). نیز is an adverb meaning 'also/too' modifying the whole phrase. خویش is a reflexive/own-marker ('themselves / their own') placed after the noun. تواند is the 3rd‑person present of توانستن 'to be able'; in classical/poetic Persian a singular-form verb can occur with a plural subject — here it means 'are able / can'.
segments.s682-g42: چو is a temporal subordinating particle 'when/as'. چوکارت is a fused expression (چو + کارت) meaning roughly 'when the matter/your task comes/when they set about it'. شود is the present/passive/become verb 'becomes' introducing the subordinate clause. سخت is an adverbial intensifier 'firmly/strongly' and پیش here functions adverbially ('forward' / 'forth'); the clause closes with another تواند ('can') as the main-modal verb applying to the subject introduced earlier. The sequence shows a subordinate time clause (چو...شود) followed by the main possibility (سخت ... تواند).
segments.s682-g43: چو + past (بشنید) is a temporal clause 'when he heard'. In the clause خسرو is the subject (Khosrow / the king); word order places the verb before or next to the subject as is common in poetry. This segment sets the time/event: 'When the king heard'.
segments.s682-g44: زمین بوس داد is a light verbal construction: بوس is the noun 'a kiss' and داد is the verb 'gave' — literally 'gave a kiss to the ground', i.e. 'kissed the earth'. بسی بر نهان preceding (بسی بر نهان آفرین کرد یاد) means 'many / in great measure' (بسی) + prepositional/locative nuance (بر نهان 'in/secretly'), آفرین is an exclamation/praise ('praise/acclamation') and کرد is the main verb 'made/did' with یاد 'remembrance' as its object: 'he offered much secret praise in remembrance.'
segments.s682-g45: A series of verbs/verb-like forms joined by و indicates successive actions. ببندوی is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning roughly 'he/you closed' or 'he shut' (optative/imperative colouring in poetry). گردوی here appears in sequence as a verbal/actional element (the written lemma lists concrete senses, but in context it functions as part of the verbal sequence 'turned/rounded'). گستهم is a first‑person (poetic) form 'I wandered/was scattered' and گفت (here used as 'said/spoke') introduces speech. In short: 'he shut/turned about and, wandering/speaking, said...'.
segments.s682-g46: که introduces reported speech 'that ...'. ما is the first‑person plural subject 'we'. با + غم و رنج is an ordinary prepositional phrase 'with sorrow and hardship'. گشتیم is the 1st‑person plural past/ perfect 'we became / we were turned' (here 'we have become'). جفت is the predicate 'paired' or 'matched' placed after the verb: 'that we have become paired with sorrow and hardship.'
segments.s682-g47: بسازید is a 2nd‑person plural imperative 'make/prepare'. و links clauses. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/altogether'. بنه here (given the lexical options) is a small bundle / set (in context it functions like 'equipment' or 'a kit' in poetic usage) and برنهید (بر + نهید) is a causative/imperative 'place upon / clothe / arm' — so the sequence is an instruction: 'prepare and wholly arm / clothe yourselves.'
segments.s682-g48: برو is an imperative 'go' (vocative/instruction). بوم ایران is the noun phrase 'the homeland / land of Iran' (بوم = native soil). بدشمن is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'to/for the evil/hostile enemy' and دهد (دهید) is the 2nd‑person plural present/imperative of 'to give' — here an instruction or bitter statement: 'go, give the land of Iran to the enemy.' Word order places the verb at the end (typical in Persian imperatives when combined).
segments.s682-g57: همی‌شد is an archaic/perfective-continuous past marker meaning ‘was beginning / kept on’; سوی روم is a prepositional phrase ‘toward Rome’. برسان is the stem/infinitive of رساندن ‘to bring / to bear’ used as a verbal complement, and گرد here is an old verbal/adverbial particle attached to that verbal cluster (literary usage) — the whole chunk expresses a past action in progress: ‘(he) was turning/bringing (it) toward Rome’.
segments.s682-g58: درفشی is the banner/standard (here functioning as the noun being borne). پس + پشت او gives the locative/relational idea ‘behind him’ (پس here ‘after/behind’ and پشت ‘rear, behind’). لاژورد is an attributive noun/colour following the phrase and describes the banner (no copula required in Persian).
segments.s682-g59: بپیچید is the simple past third person of پیچیدن with the prefix بـ (a common classical/perfective form) meaning ‘twisted/tossed/turned’. The coordinated list یال و بر و روی is the definite object (را appears later) enumerating parts ('the mane and the breast/neck/side and the face'), with و serving as the conjunction between items; را marks the whole list as definite object of the verb.
segments.s682-g60: نگه کرد is a compound verb: نگه (a noun/nominal meaning ‘holding, restraint, watch’) + کرد (past of کردن) = ‘he held / kept / checked’. The objects گستهم و بند وی را follow, joined by و and marked by را; بند وی ‘his band/strap/rein’ is the direct object. (گستهم is a literary/poetic form here functioning as an object in the pair ‘گستهم و بند’ — contextually a part of the horse’s harness/girth.)
segments.s682-g61: همی‌راندند is an iterative/continuous past verb ‘they were riding/driving’ (همی- marking repeated or continuous action, راندند past 3pl of راندن). آن دو تن is the nominative subject ‘those two persons’; نرم نرم is a reduplicated adverbial ‘softly / gently / slowly’ modifying the verb.
segments.s682-g62: خروشید is a past verb meaning ‘(he) roared / cried aloud’. خسرو is the subject/name following it. به آوای گرم is an instrumental/adverbial phrase: به used adverbially (‘with / in’), آوای ‘voice’, and گرم an adjective ‘warm / fervent’, so the phrase means ‘in a warm/fervent voice.’
segments.s682-g63: همانا is an emphatic/expository particle ‘verily / behold’. سران تان is the topical/subject phrase ‘your chiefs/leaders’ (تان = your). ز پیش آمدست is a classical-perfect phrase: ز پیش ‘before/in advance’ + آمدست (archaic perfect ‘has come’) = ‘had come beforehand / are already here’.
segments.s682-g64: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause ‘that…’. بدخواه تان is ‘an ill-wisher / enemy of yours’ (تان = your). همچو خویش آمدست is a simile: همچو ‘like/as’, خوی + ش = خویش ‘his own’ (lit. his disposition/one of his own), and آمدست ‘has come’; together, ‘has come like one of his own / as if he were one of you.’
segments.s682-g81: هم آنگه queuing before a subordinate clause means 'even then/at that very moment'; که introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. او خویشتن کرد is literal 'he did himself' — خویشتن is a reflexive/emphatic object and کرد is simple past 'did/acted' (poetic word order places the predicate راست after the verb). راست is a predicate/adverb meaning 'rightly/straight' describing how he acted. The whole segment is a temporal clause: 'even then, when he himself acted rightly.'
segments.s682-g82: نژندی و وکژی are two coordinated nouns 'baseness and crookedness' (و links them). ازین is the fused/prepositional form 'از + این' meaning 'from this/of this'; بهر here means 'for/toward' or 'from the side of', and ماست is the copula 'is (for us)' — ست is the archaic present copula attached to ما. The clause states that these two evils 'from this source are (upon) us.'
segments.s682-g83: چو آید introduces a temporal clause 'when he comes' (چو = 'when'). بران مرز = 'to that border/frontier' (بران = 'to/upon that'). بندش کنید is an imperative: بند( + ش ) + کنید — the -ش is the third-person object clitic 'him/it' and کنید is the plural imperative 'do/perform' (here: 'bind/close him/it'). Word order places the target (بران مرز) before the verb in poetic style.
segments.s682-g84: دل شادمان را marks the definite object 'the rejoicing/joyful heart' with را (object marker). گزندش کنید is another imperative 'do harm' built from گزند ('harm') plus the clitic -ش (again 'it/him') and کنید ('do' imperative). The construction literally orders: 'Make harm (to) the joyful heart.'
segments.s682-g85: بدین is the archaic contracted prepositional phrase 'به + این' meaning 'to this'. بارگاهش is 'court/palace' with the possessive clitic -ش 'his'. فرستید is the plural imperative 'send', and باز is an adverb 'back/again' placed after the verb. Together: 'Send him back to this court.'
segments.s682-g86: ممانید here is an imperative meaning 'do not remain/don't stay' (negative injunction). تا introduces a temporal clause 'until'; گردد is the subjunctive/present-form 'become' (used as 'becomes'), او is the third-person pronoun 'he', and سرفراز is the predicative adjective 'victorious/proud'. The clause means 'do not stay until he becomes victorious.'
segments.s682-g87: ببندید is the plural imperative 'close/shut' (often applied to gates); هم is an adverb here ('also/indeed'—emphatic), در زمان means 'at the time' → 'promptly/at once', and با سپاه is the prepositional phrase 'with the army'. The phrase orders immediate closing/action together with the army.
segments.s682-g88: فرستید is the plural imperative 'send'. گریان functions as a participial/adverbial modifier 'weeping' (manner: 'send (them) weeping' or 'send (the) weeping ones'). بدین جایگاه is again 'به + این جایگاه' = 'to this place' (here split as ب + دین in the segment); جایگاه is 'place'. The segment directs: 'Send (them) weeping to this place.'
segments.s682-g1: وزان (from/and from that) is a deictic particle pointing back to a previously mentioned place or moment; جایگه (place) is the noun it qualifies. شد is the past/resultative verb 'became / came to be' used here intransitively to mean 'went / came' into a location. به پیش is a prepositional phrase ('to / into the presence of') and پدر is the noun 'father' marked as the person whose presence is entered.
segments.s682-g2: This is a compact adjectival/descriptive phrase. دودیده is a descriptive adjective (literally 'two‑eyed' in the sense of having both eyes visible, here poetically implying tearful eyes). پراز is an older/poetic form meaning 'full of' and governs the following nouns; آب ('water', here metonymically 'tears') and پر خون جگر is a parallel construction where پر ('full (of)') again modifies خون جگر ('blood of the liver/heart') — a conventional Persian idiom for deep internal grief. The two halves are linked by و 'and'.
segments.s682-g3: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. روی پدر is the object of دید ('saw') with the usual noun‑noun order 'face of the father'. بردش نماز is an idiomatic, compact clause: برد (from بُردن 'to take') with the enclitic object ش ('him') and نماز ('prayer') placed after the verb — here the sense is 'prayer took him' or 'he was seized/moved by prayer', i.e. he began to pray or was overwhelmed by devotion. The verb takes the clitic object directly (برد + ش).
segments.s682-g4: همی‌بود is an imperfect/continuous past form meaning 'continued to be / remained'. پیشش uses پیش with the pronominal clitic ش to mean 'in his presence / before him'. زمانی دراز is an adverbial phrase 'for a long time' (زمانی 'for a while' + دراز 'long'), placed after the verb to indicate duration.
segments.s682-g5: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (به + او contracted), placed before the reporting verb; گفت is the reporting verb 'said'. کاین is the demonstrative 'this' (ک + این) introducing the following noun phrase; پهلوان سوار is a compound noun phrase 'champion / heroic rider' (پهلوان qualified by سوار). The word order is typical: indirect object ('to him') + verb + the noun phrase being pointed out.
segments.s682-g6: که introduces a relative clause 'that / whom'. او را is the object 'him' with the Persian definite/object marker را. گزین is the verbal root 'choose' and کردی is the 2nd person past 'you did/chose' (here followed by the vocative ای شهریار 'O king'); the vocative follows the verb in this older/poetic syntax. So the clause reads 'whom you chose, O king.'
segments.s682-g7: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came'. چوشاهان here represents چو + شاهان and functions like 'as/when the kings' or 'then the kings' — an archaic/conventional poetic phrasing. که دارد / دارند فر is a relative clause 'who have/possess farr' (فر = the divine/royal splendour); دارند is the present/historical-present plural 'they have' describing those kings.
segments.s682-g8: سپاهی بیاورد is a verbal phrase 'brought an army' (سپاهی 'soldiers/army' as object, بیاورد 'brought' / 'would bring' depending on poetic tense). بسیارمر is an adverbial intensifier meaning 'very much / in great number' (poetic compound), modifying the bringing; note that classical word order and poetic compression can place the object (سپاهی) before the verb.
segments.s682-g73: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction) introducing a subordinate temporal clause. چوبینه here is a noun ('the wooden thing' or 'club/Chubīneh' — a poetic or possibly proper-name usage) and آید is the simple present/poetic present 'comes' (third-person singular). بایوان is an archaic/obscure item here (lexically uncertain) that sits next to شاه 'king' — the phrase as a whole means 'when the (wooden) chubīneh comes to the king' with an unusual ordering typical of epic verse.
segments.s682-g74: هم آنگه is an emphatic temporal/temporal‑adverbial phrase ('right then', 'at once'); به is the preposition 'to/for', هرمز is the divine name 'Hormoz/Ahura Mazda', دهد is the present/poetic verb 'gives', and تاج وگاه is a compound object 'crown and (seat/throne/time of rule)' — together: 'he at once gives the crown and throne to Hormoz.'
segments.s682-g75: نشیند is the poetic/present form 'he sits' (3rd sg.); چو introduces the subordinate temporal clause 'when', دستور is 'command/order', بردست is a prepositional/agentive phrase 'in/by his hand' and اوی is the 3rd‑person pronoun 'his/he'. Word order (verb–subordinate‑clause) is typical of verse: 'He sits when the command is in his hand.'
segments.s682-g76: بدریا is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the sea', رسد is the simple present/poetic 'reaches/arrives', کارگر here is adjectival/epic diction meaning 'active/effective', شست in classical usage can mean a part of the hand (here used poetically) and اوی is 'his/he'; together the clause says '[his] effective hand reaches the sea' (a poetic way to say his power reaches to the sea).
segments.s682-g77: بقیصر is the prepositional compound 'to Caesar' (بـ + قیصر). یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (colloquial/epic 'one/a'), نامه is 'letter', از is the preposition 'from', and شهریار is 'the king' — literally 'to Caesar a certain letter from the king'.
segments.s682-g78: نویسد is the narrative/poetic present 'he writes' (3rd sg.); که introduces reported content 'that', این 'this' points to the person described, بنده is the humble/formal 'servant' or 'subject', and نابکار is an epithet 'wicked' — together introducing the reported clause: 'he writes that this wicked servant...'.
segments.s682-g79: گریزان is an active/adjectival participle 'fleeing' or 'having fled', برفتست is the perfect/poetic 'has gone/has departed', زین here functions as a prepositional 'from this' (archaic spelling/usage), مرز is 'border', and وبوم is 'and the homeland/land' — 'having fled from this border and land.'
segments.s682-g80: نباید is the impersonal/modal 'it must not / one must not', that-clause marker که introduces the subordinate clause, آرام 'calm/peace', گیرد is the present/poetic subjunctive/optative 'take/become' used idiomatically with آرام (آرام گیرد = 'to find rest / to be at peace'), and بروم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive 'I go' often used in constructions meaning 'wherever I go' or 'as I go'; together it expresses 'he must not be permitted to find rest wherever I go' (a forceful negative wish/warning).
segments.s682-g65: این عبارت ترکیبی شرطی/پرسشی است: 'اگر نه' = 'if not' (a short conditional), followed by the verbal-noun/infinitive 'چنین نرم راندن' ('to ride/drive thus gently'). 'چراست' is an interrogative copula meaning 'why is it?' (poetic/archaic form). Word order is inverted for rhetorical effect: the infinitive phrase is the topic and 'چراست' closes the question.
segments.s682-g66: 'که' introduces the explanatory clause ('for/since/that'), and the clause is a normal predication: subject 'بهرام' + locative/adverb 'نزدیک پشت شما' + copula 'ست' (poetic form of 'است'). 'پشت شما' = 'at your rear/behind you'.
segments.s682-g67: Word order is inverted and poetic: 'بدو' = 'to him' (indirect object), 'گفت' is the past 3rd-person of 'to say' (here 'said'), and 'بندوی' is the subject 'the bondman'. 'کای شهریار' is a vocative ('O king') addressing the addressee.
segments.s682-g68: 'دلت را' = 'your heart' (دل + 2nd-person suffix ت + object marker را). 'ببهرام' = 'for/to Bahram' (به + بهرام) indicating the one who might cause distress. 'رنجه مدار' is a poetic prohibition: 'رنجه' (be afflicted/troubled) + 'مدار' functioning as a negative imperative ('do not ...'); together: 'do not let (your) heart be troubled.'
segments.s682-g69: 'کجا' opens a rhetorical question ('how/where'), 'گرد' here works adverbially ('around/about'), 'ما را' is the object 'us' (marked by را), and 'نبیند' is negative present/subjunctive/epistolary 3rd-person of 'to see' used rhetorically ('would he not see'). 'ز راه' = 'from the road'. The clause asks/assumes that seeing from the road is inevitable.
segments.s682-g70: 'که' here gives the reason ('for/since'), 'دورست' = 'is distant' (predicate), 'ز ایدر' = 'from here' (poetic 'ایدَر' = here), and 'درفش سیاه' = 'the black banner' (noun + postpositive adjective). The clause explains why they should be seen or noticed.
segments.s682-g71: 'چنین است' = 'thus it is' or 'so it is' introducing reported speech. 'یارانت را' marks the indirect object 'to your companions'. 'گفت و گوی' is a compact poetic expression meaning 'said and spoke' / 'utterance' (here: he addressed them). Word order is elevated/elliptical as typical in epic verse.
segments.s682-g72: 'که' introduces the content clause ('that'). 'ما را' is the experiencer/object 'we' (with را). 'بدین تاختن' = 'to attack in this way' (بدین = 'in this manner', formed from prefix بـ + demonstrative; 'تاختن' = 'to charge/attack'). 'نیست روی' literally 'there is no face/courage' — idiomatically 'we have no heart/courage' to do that; 'نیست' negates the copula. The overall meaning: 'we lack the heart to attack thus.'
segments.s682-g9: بگفتم is a past 1st‑person verb (‘I said’). سخن is the head noun ‘words/speech’ and is followed by the relative/indefinite clause هرچ آمد ز پند (‘whatever came from advice’), where هرچ (= هر چه) = ‘whatever’, آمد is simple past ‘came/was uttered’, and ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/as’. The whole unit means ‘I said whatever words came as advice.’
segments.s682-g10: برو is the imperative ‘go’. پندِ من is a genitive phrase ‘my counsel/advice’ (من used possessively after the noun). بر نبد سودمند is an archaic negative copular/existential phrase: نبد (classical variant of نبود) = ‘was not / did not exist’ with بر = ‘upon/for’; سودمند is the adjective ‘useful’. Together: ‘go; my counsel will not be useful.’
segments.s682-g11: همه جنگ و پرخاش is the noun phrase ‘all (his) wars and assaults’. بدکام is an adjectival predicate ‘ill‑fated/unsuccessful’. اوی is a pronominal clitic referring to ‘him’ (dative/possessive flavor), so the clause means ‘all his wars and assaults were ill‑fated for him.’ Word order places the predicate after the coordinated subject, typical of Ferdowsi’s diction.
segments.s682-g12: که introduces a subordinate/result clause (‘so that/that’). هرگز مبادا is an emphatic negative optative ‘may it never be’. روان here functions adjectivally/adverbially with نام to indicate continuity/flowing sense (an older usage conveying the idea of an ongoing reputation), and نام اوی = ‘his name/reputation’. The clause therefore expresses the wish ‘may his name/reputation never cease/be undone.’
segments.s682-g13: This is a passive/resultive construction. بنکام (بـِناکام) = ‘unsuccessful/frustrated’ functions as predicative; رزمی گران is a compound referring to the fighters/warriors (رزمی = ‘martial’ + گران an archaic agentive/collective element), and کرده شد is the periphrastic passive (‘was made’, with کرده as the participle and شد as auxiliary). So: ‘the martial fighters were made unsuccessful/vanquished.’
segments.s682-g14: فراوان کس = ‘many a person / many people’ (focusing the plurality). از اختر آزرده شد is a passive/experiential clause: از = ‘because of / by (the agency of)’, اختر = ‘the star’ (astrological influence), آزرده شد = ‘was distressed / afflicted’. Together: ‘many a man was distressed by the star.’
segments.s682-g15: ز من is a prepositional phrase ‘from me / away from me’ (ز = poetic ‘from’ + من = ‘me’). بازگشتند is 3pl past ‘they returned/turned back’. یکسر is an adverb ‘entirely / all at once’ modifying the verb, and سپاه is the subject ‘the army/host’. Classical Persian often places the prepositional phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s682-g16: ندیدند is 3pl past negative ‘they did not see / they did not meet’. The remainder is a compressed poetic object phrase: گفتی ... مرا را / جز به راه — here the object ‘مرا’ (me) and the object marker را are distributed by the verse’s word order, and جز به راه = ‘except on the road’. The whole clause conveys ‘they did not see/meet me except on the road.’ (The exact placement of گفتی/م/را reflects poetic contraction; the natural prose order would be: آنها مرا جز به راه ندیدند.)
segments.s682-g121: بدانست is the past tense verb 'he realized/understood.' کایشان is an archaic contracted relative 'که ایشان' = 'that they' linking the subordinate clause. دو دل literally 'two‑heart' describes a state of wavering or having two minds; پر ز is a poetic, inverted way to say 'full of' with ز = 'of/from.' راز is the noun 'secret.'
segments.s682-g122: چرا is the interrogative 'why' introducing the reason; از marks source 'from' or 'away from' (poetic use). جهاندار means 'the sovereign/king.' گشتند is the past plural of گشتن used here in the sense 'turned/changed direction.' باز is an adverb 'back/away' (here 'turned back'). Together they form 'why they turned back from the sovereign.'
segments.s682-g123: برخساره شد is a compound meaning roughly 'was stricken with loss/ruined' (برخساره as a stative/nominal element plus شد 'became'). چون introduces a simile 'like/as.' گل شنبلید names the comparator — 'the fenugreek flower' (شنبلید is the plant/flower word used poetically).
segments.s682-g124: نکرد is the negative past of کردن used in the sense 'did not cause / did not do'; آن سخن 'that speech/utterance' is the direct object; بر دلیران literally 'upon the brave' (i.e. toward the warriors); پدید is 'to appear/manifest.' The clause means 'that speech did not become manifest to the brave.'
segments.s682-g125: بدیشان is a prepositional phrase 'to them.' چنین گفت is a fixed expression 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت here stands for 'said'). کزشاه is an elided relative 'که شاه' = 'that the king/from the king.' راه ('road/way') begins the content reported in the following segment.
segments.s682-g126: بگردید is a past/plural form of گشتن used here in the sense 'turned/changed (course).' کامد is an archaic present/past form meaning 'came/arrived' (here 'was coming/entered'); بتنگی is 'narrowness' or 'a tight place' and سپاه is 'the army.' The clause says the army was entering a narrow/difficult situation after the king changed course.
segments.s682-g127: بیابان گزینید is an imperative instruction 'choose the desert' (گزینید here understood as an imperative). وراه is the conjunction + noun 'and the road'; دراز is the adjective 'long' modifying راه. The speaker is advising to take the wilderness rather than a long route.
segments.s682-g128: مدارید is an imperative/ hortative sense 'be lenient' / 'spare' (here directed at the addressees). یکسر 'entirely' qualifies the following phrase. تن 'body' and از رنج 'from suffering'; باز here functions as 'free/spared' (i.e. keep your bodies entirely free from suffering). The clause is an injunction to spare oneself from hardship.
segments.s682-g113: هم آنگاه functions as a temporal adverbial ('just then/at that moment'); هم here is an intensifier ('even/just') and آنگاه = 'then'. برخاست is a past tense intransitive verb 'arose/rose up' and follows Persian SOV tendencies here with its adverbial foregrounded. آواز کوس is a noun phrase ('the voice/sound of the drum') in which آواز (sound/voice) is qualified by کوس (war-drum).
segments.s682-g114: رخ خونیان گشت is a small clause where رخ ('face/appearance') is the topic and گشت ('became/turned') is the copular/result verb (past). خونیان is an adjectival/participial description meaning 'blood-stained/bloody' modifying رخ. چون introduces the simile 'like/as' and سندروس (a fragrant/red wood) is the comparison term.
segments.s682-g115: درفش سپهبد is a genitival juxtaposition meaning 'the spahbed's banner/standard' (Persian often places possessor after the possessed). هم آنگه is an adverbial phrase 'also/at that time', ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and راه is the source 'road/way' (z + noun = 'from the road').
segments.s682-g116: پدید آمد is a compound verbal expression where پدید (appear) combines with آمد (came) to mean 'appeared/emerged'. اندر میان سپاه is a locative phrase ('into/within the middle of the army')—اندر = 'in/within', میان = 'middle', سپاه = 'army'. Word order places the verb before the locative phrase for rhythm/poetic effect.
segments.s682-g117: جفا پیشه is a compound noun 'those who practice cruelty' (i.e. 'the cruel ones'). گستهم is an archaic/poetic past form (lemma given as 'I wandered') appearing here in the narrative; in practice it functions as part of a coordinated clause with the following conjunction و. بند وی تیز is a noun phrase where بند = 'bond/fetter/cord' and وی is a third‑person pronoun 'his/their/that', while تیز here works adverbially/adjectivally ('quick/firm') describing how the bond is handled; the full clause links to the verbal action in the next segment.
segments.s682-g118: گرفتند is a past-tense transitive verb 'they seized/took' (3rd person plural). زان is a fused/prepositional deictic 'from that' pointing back to the palace; کاخ = 'palace'. راه گریز is a compound noun 'the way/path of escape' (راه = way, گریز = escape), the direct object of گرفتند. Persian allows the object phrase to appear after the verb for poetic rhythm.
segments.s682-g119: چنین تا introduces a temporal clause 'thus until/so that' where تا + بخسرو ('to the king' — بخسرو = بـ + خسرو) marks the extent/destination. رسید is the past tense verb 'arrived/reached'. این دومرد is a noun phrase 'this two-man' or 'these two men' (دومرد = 'two men' or 'a pair of men') placed after the clause as topic/continuation.
segments.s682-g120: جهانجوی is an appositive or epitheton meaning 'the world‑seeking / ambitious one'. چون + دیدشان forms a subordinate temporal clause 'when/when he saw them' (چون here introducing the temporal clause). روی زرد is a verbless/result clause common in Persian: 'his face (became) pale' — the copula 'گشت/شد' is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s682-g49: بگفت = an archaic past verb form (بـ- prefix + گفتن) meaning 'he said'; این = 'this' (the preceding utterance or thing); و = coordinating 'and/and then'; از دیده آواز خاست is a small clause where از + دیده marks the source ('from [his] eye(s)') and آواز (noun) is the thing that خاست (from خاستن, 'arose'). Word order is verb (بگفت) then the following clause introduced by و; خاست is simple past 'arose'.
segments.s682-g50: که‌ای here introduces the quoted exclamation: که (complementizer/quotative) + ای (vocative 'O'), so the phrase begins a direct address 'O king'. شاه is the noun 'king'; the following words are epithets in coordination: نیک (good/auspicious), اختر (literally 'star', a poetic epithet), و = 'and', داد = the noun 'justice', وراست (وراست) = 'upright' or 'right.' The sequence lists attributes of the addressee; word order is vocative + noun + attributive nouns/adjectives.
segments.s682-g51: یکی + گرد تیره forms a nominal phrase 'a certain/one round dark [thing]'; یکی is an indefinite numeral/determiner 'a certain / one', گرد There denotes a round shape ('round'/'disc') and تیره is the adjective 'dark'. برآمد is the past verb 'arose/emerged' and ز راه is a prepositional phrase 'from the road' indicating source. Word order: determiner + noun + adjective, then verb, then source phrase.
segments.s682-g52: درفشی درفشان is a tautological/poetic pairing where درفشی (banner/standard) is qualified by درفشان ('shining, glittering'); this yields 'a shining banner'. میان سپاه is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in the midst of the army/host' (میان = middle/among, سپاه = army). The noun phrase follows the previous clause and specifies what emerged.
segments.s682-g53: This is a relative-description: درفشی (the banner) is followed by a relative marker کجا here functioning as 'in which/whose' (a poetic/archaic usage), then پیکرش is پیکر + third-person possessive suffix -ش 'its body/figure', and اژدهاست is 'is a dragon' (اژدها + است). So the clause reads 'a banner in which/whose figure was a dragon.'
segments.s682-g54: که is the relative 'which/that' linking to the banner; چوبینه is an adjectival/nominal form meaning 'wooden' or 'a wooden (staff/pole)' (here describing the banner's staff); بر نهروان is a prepositional phrase 'toward Nahrawān' (بر used here as 'toward/to'); کرد راست is a compound of کرد (past of کردن 'to make/set') + راست 'straight/upright', so 'set upright.' Together: 'which had a wooden staff set upright toward Nahrawān.'
segments.s682-g55: چوبشنید is a fused archaic clause (چو + بشنید) meaning 'when he heard that / on hearing it'; خسرو is the proper name 'Khosrow'; بیامد is past 'came' (bi- prefix + آمد); بدر here is an epithet 'Badr'/'full moon' used appositionally to praise the arrival. Word order is temporal clause + subject + verb + epithet.
segments.s682-g56: گریزان is a present-participle/adjectival form 'fleeing' that expresses manner; برفت is an archaic past verb meaning 'he went off / departed / fled' (بر- prefix before رفتن), followed by the pronoun او 'he' in postverbal position for emphasis. ز پیش پدر is a prepositional phrase 'from before his father' (ز = from, پیش = before/in the presence of, پدر = father). The segment thus means 'he fled away from before his father.'
segments.s682-g89: چنین (an adverb) modifies the whole reply: 'thus/so'. داد is a past tense verb 'gave' used with the noun پاسخ 'answer' (the light-weight compound 'دادِ پاسخ' = 'gave an answer / answered'). که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. از بخت بد is a causal prepositional phrase: از + بخت ('fortune') + بد ('bad'), literally 'because of bad fortune.'
segments.s682-g90: سزد is an impersonal/present judgment verb meaning 'it befits / it is proper'. زین is an archaic form (زِ + این) meaning 'by/from this' and here governs نشان 'sign/token' (زین نشان = 'by this sign'). هرچ بر ما رسد is a relative/free‑subordinate clause: هرچ ('whatever') + بر ('upon') + ما ('us') + رسد (present/subjunctive 'befalls / may come to'), so 'whatever may befall us.'
segments.s682-g91: سخنها is the plural noun 'words/speeches' (سخن + plural -ها). درازست is an adjectival predicate (درَاز + است) meaning 'is long' — Persian allows adjective + است as a single predicate. و links the two clauses, and کاری درشت is a nominal phrase: کاری ('a matter/deed') + درشت ('serious/large/coarse'), so 'and the matter is grave.'
segments.s682-g92: به یزدان is an oath-like prepositional phrase 'by God' (به + یزدان). کنون is an adverb 'now.' باز هشتیم پشت is an older/poetic word order: باز 'again' + هشتیم is a dialectal/poetic variant here functioning as 'we are / we have' (classical spelling differs), and پشت 'back' (i.e. 'we turn our backs' or 'we are turned back'). Together: 'by God, now we again turn our backs.'
segments.s682-g93: براند (a past form of راندن) is 'he drove' or 'he spurred on' and takes اسپ 'horse' as its object (براند اسپ = 'he rode/drove the horse'). وگفت 'and said' connects the action to speech. آنچ is a relative pronoun 'that which / what' and از here means 'about/concerning' (آنچ از خوب و زشت = 'what concerning good and evil'). خوب و زشت form the pair 'good and bad/evil.'
segments.s682-g94: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign/king.' برتارک is a locative/phrase meaning 'on the brow / upon the forehead' (برتارک = بر + تارک in older usage). ما is the object 'our' (here 'on our brow'). نبشت is a past tense verb 'wrote/inscribed' (نیشت/نبشت = wrote), so 'the sovereign wrote/inscribed (it) on our brow.'
segments.s682-g95: بباشد is an optative/jussive form meaning 'let it be / may it be.' نگردد is a negative jussive/optative of گشتن 'to turn/ become' meaning 'may it not turn' or 'do not let it turn.' باندیشه is a prepositional/adverbial compound 'in (one's) thought / in mind' (بـ + اندیشه). باز here means 'again' — overall: 'may it be so; let it not again turn in thought.'
segments.s682-g96: مبادا is a protective/exhortative particle 'lest / may it not be' used to introduce a feared consequence. که is the subordinating conjunction 'that/that...' and آید is present/subjunctive of آمدن 'come' ('should come'). بدشمن is 'hostile/evil enemy' and نیاز here means 'need' / 'time of necessity' — together: 'lest, God forbid, a hostile enemy should arrive in a time of need.'

sentences.s682-s17: By this deed God was your support, and fortune smiled in accord with you.
sentences.s682-s18: When he left (or abandoned it), if you will, (abandon) the border and the homeland; from here go to the Arabs until I go.
sentences.s682-s19: Make known the words of this humble servant who seeks a remedy; when you go, tell each one to the Caesar.
sentences.s682-s20: To the place where religion exists and also where desire (is) — his weapon and army are arranged.
sentences.s682-s49: When he passed by, the two tyrants turned back from him, their heads full of hatred.
sentences.s682-s50: They came from the road into the king's iwan; they arrived full of suffering and with sinful hearts.
sentences.s682-s51: When they came from the gate to near the throne, they cried 'zehî' and fiercely drew their bows.
sentences.s682-s52: Suddenly they cast (a noose) about his neck and hanged that noble body of his.
sentences.s682-s53: That crown and that throne of the emperors came to be; you said that Hurmuz did not exist in the world.
sentences.s682-s54: Thus is the custom of the revolving age: sometimes it brings delight, at other times poison.
sentences.s682-s55: If this is the source, do not seek its profit; for in seeking it hardship will come upon you.
sentences.s682-s56: When the wheel of days comes to Hurmuz's turn, that fortunate throne's place will remain empty.
sentences.s682-s13: If, O base one, the sovereign gives the command, I will bring/send countless Arab horsemen.
sentences.s682-s14: Hormoz said to him that this counsel is not (right), that now you have no firm footing.
sentences.s682-s15: Let the Arabs not be your allies; wherever they do not perceive profit or loss.
sentences.s682-s16: They also hand over your wretched inner heart to a hostile enemy for the sake of some thing (i.e. over a trifle).
sentences.s682-s9: They kept calling Bahram "king"; they did not see the beginning of the end.
sentences.s682-s10: So now I will go as far as the Nahrawan bridge; the army comes like a heavy mountain.
sentences.s682-s11: When the affair became fruitless I fled; I did not fall into the snare of calamity.
sentences.s682-s12: I weighed now the profit and the loss; there is no helper except the Arabs.

sentences.s682-s22: When the king heard it, Khosrow kissed the earth.
sentences.s682-s23: He wrapped up and turned and spoke (out), saying that we have become paired with sorrow and hardship.
sentences.s682-s24: Prepare (yourselves) and put on (your arms) completely; go and give the homeland of Iran to the enemy.

sentences.s682-s31: Those two were riding/driving gently; Khosrow burst forth with a warm voice.
sentences.s682-s32: Truly your chiefs have already come forward; your enemy has come like one of your own.

sentences.s682-s42: When he comes to that border, bind him; harm the rejoicing heart.
sentences.s682-s43: Send him back to this court, and do not stay until he becomes triumphant.
sentences.s682-s44: Shut it fast at once; with the army, send (them) weeping to this place.
sentences.s682-s1: From that place he went into his father's presence, his two eyes full of water and his heart full of blood.
sentences.s682-s2: When he saw his father's face, prayer seized him; he remained before him for a long while.
sentences.s682-s3: He said to him, 'This champion rider whom you have chosen, O king...'
sentences.s682-s4: Then came, as kings, those who possess farr (royal glory), and they brought a great army.
sentences.s682-s37: When the wooden club (or Chubīneh) came, the king at once gave the crown and throne to Hormoz.
sentences.s682-s38: He sits when the command is in his hand; his (effective) hand reaches the sea.
sentences.s682-s39: To Caesar, a certain letter from the king was written, saying that 'this wicked servant...'
sentences.s682-s40: He has fled from this border and land; he must not be allowed to rest wherever he goes.
sentences.s682-s33: If not, why this gentle riding — for Bahram is close at your back?
sentences.s682-s34: The bondman said to him, 'O king, do not let your heart be troubled by Bahram.'
sentences.s682-s35: How could he not see us from the road, since the black banner is far from here?
sentences.s682-s36: Thus he told your companions, saying that we have no heart to make such an attack.
sentences.s682-s5: I said: whatever words came as counsel — go; my advice will not be of any use.
sentences.s682-s6: All his wars and assaults proved ill‑fated for him; may his reputation never be undone.
sentences.s682-s7: The warriors were rendered unsuccessful; many a man, because of the star, was distressed.
sentences.s682-s8: The whole army at once turned back from me; they did not see/meet me except on the road.

sentences.s682-s62: He was stricken with ruin like a fenugreek blossom; that speech did not appear to the brave.
sentences.s682-s63: He said to them thus: that the king had changed course and the army was coming into a narrow strait.
sentences.s682-s64: Choose the desert and avoid the long road; spare your bodies entirely from suffering.
sentences.s682-s57: At that moment the sound of the war-drum arose; their faces turned blood-red like sandalwood.
sentences.s682-s58: At the same time the spahbed's standard appeared from the road and came into the midst of the army.
sentences.s682-s59: The cruel ones closed in and they seized, from that palace, the road of escape.
sentences.s682-s60: Thus, until he reached the king, when the world-seeking man saw them his face turned pale.
sentences.s682-s25: He said this, and from his eyes a voice rose: "O king — good, a star, just and upright!"

sentences.s682-s27: A banner whose figure was a dragon, whose wooden staff was set upright toward Nahrawān.
sentences.s682-s28: On hearing that, Khosrow (like the moon) came; he fled, departing from before his father.
sentences.s682-s45: He answered thus: "Because of ill fortune, it is fitting — by this sign — whatever may befall us."
sentences.s682-s46: The words are long and the matter is grave; by God, now we again turn our backs.

sentences.s682-s48: Let it be so; do not again turn back in thought — lest, God forbid, a hostile enemy should come in need.
